segments.s729-g25: سپه را: 'سپه' (army) is marked by the accusative marker 'را' (object marker) so the phrase is the direct object. 'به در خواند' is a light-verb construction where 'خواند' (past tense 'he called/summoned') takes the prepositional phrase 'به در' (to the gate; here 'در' = gate) as its complement. The conjunction 'و' links this clause to the next; the whole clause is a simple past narrative action.
segments.s729-g26: چو شد روز روشن: 'چو' = 'when'; 'شد' is past of 'شدن' (became), so 'when the day became bright/dawned'. 'بنه برنهاد' is a poetic compound: 'بنه' (pack/baggage) is the object and 'برنهاد' (past of برنهادن, literally 'lifted/placed up') is the transitive verb meaning 'he lifted/put up' (i.e., prepared to set out). Word order is typical Persian: temporal clause (چو شد ...) followed by the main action.
segments.s729-g27: چو آمد : 'چو' = 'when' and 'آمد' is past tense 'he came'. 'به نزدیکی شهریار' is a prepositional phrase 'to/near the proximity of the shah' (نزدیکی = nearness, شهریار = king); word order is verb then its locative complement introduced by 'به'.
segments.s729-g28: سپاهی پذیره شدش بی‌شمار: 'سپاهی' (troops) is the subject; 'پذیره' (reception/acceptance) here functions as a noun used in a passive/nominal predication. 'شدش' is past 'befell him/was to him' (an older poetic clitic form combining 'شد' + enclitic 'ش' = 'to him/for him'), so the phrase conveys that he was met/received by troops. 'بی‌شمار' ('countless') is a postpositive adjective emphasizing number. The clause is compact and typical of epic Persian compression.
segments.s729-g29: ز ره چون بدرگاه شد بار یافت: 'ز ره' (from the road) is a prepositional/ablative phrase (poetic 'ز' = 'from'); 'چون' = 'when'; 'بدرگاه شد' = 'he reached the court' (بدرگاه = into the royal presence/court, شد = became/arrived). 'بار یافت' literally 'found a load' (بار = load/cargo; یافت = found/obtained) — a compact past narrative: upon arriving at court he encountered/obtained a load.
segments.s729-g30: دل تاجور پر ز تیمار یافت: 'دل تاجور' = 'the merchant's heart' (دل = heart; تاجور = merchant/trader). 'پر ز تیمار' = 'full of care/worry' (پر = full (of), ز = 'of/from', تیمار = care). 'یافت' (found) here is used idiomatically to mean 'became' or 'fell into' — 'the merchant's heart was found full of care', i.e. 'the merchant's heart was filled with anxiety.'
segments.s729-g31: بیاورد زان پس نثاری گران: 'بیاورد' is past/future-subjunctive form used narratively as 'he brought' or 'he produced' (here past narrative). 'زان پس' = 'after that/thereafter' (زان = 'from/that', پس = 'after/then'). 'نثاری گران' = 'costly/valuable offerings' where نثاری is the noun 'nathar/offerings' (poetic/archaic) and گران qualifies them as 'precious/costly'. Word order: verb + temporal phrase + object phrase.
segments.s729-g32: هر آنکس که بودند با اوسران: 'هر آنکس که' = 'each/every one who' (هر = every, آنکس که = that person who). 'بودند' is past plural 'they were' (here relative clause marker structure: 'each person who was/stood...'). 'با اوسران' = 'with the chiefs/leaders' (با = with; اوسران = chiefs/respected elders). The whole relative clause modifies 'هر آنکس' to mean 'each person who was with the chiefs'.
segments.s729-g49: This clause ends with the past-tense verb افگند (afgand, classical past of افکندن) in 3rd-person singular, so the action is 'he threw/cast.' Word order places the possessor-marked noun بیارانش ('his companions') before the prepositional phrase بر خلعت ('upon a robe'), so the whole reads 'he cast the robe upon his companions.' The final نیز ('also/likewise') links this action to the following gifts, indicating addition rather than contrast.
segments.s729-g50: درم داد is a simple past clause with داد ('gave') as the 3rd-person singular past verb and درم ('dirham') as its object placed before the verb (object–verb order is common in Persian poetry). The conjunction و ('and') strings دینار ('dinar') and the phrase هرگونه چیز ('every/any kind of thing') into the list of items given; هرگونه modifies چیز to mean 'every kind of thing' or 'all sorts of things.'
segments.s729-g1: This is an object–verb–subject ordering common in poetry: 'دوات و قلم' (objects) + past verb 'خواست' + subject phrase 'ناباک زن'. 'ناباک' is an adjectival phrase modifying 'زن' (the woman). The past tense verb خواست (wanted) applies to the woman as subject.
segments.s729-g2: انداخت is a past tense verb 'threw/cast (off)'. ز هرگونه is an adverbial/prepositional phrase 'from/of every kind', and با رای زن marks the means 'by the counsel/advice of the woman'. The subject (the woman) is carried over from the previous clause and is not repeated here.
segments.s729-g3: یکی introduces an indefinite subject 'a certain person'. بنوشت here is the past tense 'wrote' (classical poetic form with the b- prefix attached to the past stem); نزدیک شاه is an adverbial phrase 'near (or in the presence of) the king'. Word order is flexible in classical Persian, so the verb follows the subject phrase.
segments.s729-g4: ز بدخواه = 'from the ill-wisher'. وز مردم is a fused form (و + ز) commonly used in classical texts meaning 'and from' the following noun; مردم نیک‌خواه = 'the people who are well-wishers'. The construction lists two sources/parties the letter comes from.
segments.s729-g5: سر نامه ('the head/opening of the letter') is a noun phrase (head + noun). کرد آفرین = 'made/put (a) praise' i.e., 'began with praise'; از نخست = 'from the first' = 'from the very beginning'. آفرین here functions as a noun 'praise/acclamation' rather than an interjection.
segments.s729-g6: بر آنکس که ... links the praise to its referent: 'for/on that person who ...'. که introduces the relative clause. او is the pronoun 'he' and کینه از دل بشست is a past-tense clause 'washed hatred from his heart' (بشست = past of شستن with the poetic b- prefix).
segments.s729-g7: دگر indicates 'another' or 'again'. گفت here is the past 'said/spoke' (the lexicon entry given is for the noun 'conversation', so the exact verbal sense is marked below as -1). کاری که فرمود شاه is a noun ('a matter/act') modified by a relative clause 'that the king commanded' (که introduces the clause; فرمود شاه places the verb before the subject, a common poetic order).
segments.s729-g8: بر آمد is an intransitive idiom 'it came about/occurred'. بکام = 'to the taste/pleasure of', دل نیک‌خواه = 'the heart of the well-wisher' (i.e., the well-wisher's satisfaction). The whole phrase states that the event happened to the liking of the well-wisher.
segments.s729-g9: پراگنده گشت is an intransitive verb phrase where the adjective/participial pra-gandeh (پراگنده, “scattered/widespread”) comes before گشت (past of گشتن, “became/was”), so together they mean “became scattered” or “was scattered.” آن سپاه سترگ is the noun phrase ‘that/the host (سپاه) great/large (سترگ)’ with آن as the determiner placed before the head noun, so the whole segment reads ‘that great/large host was scattered.’
segments.s729-g10: به introduces an agentive/causal notion here (’by/through’), governing بخت (‘fortune/fate’). جهاندار شاه بزرگ is a chain of epithets/titles modifying the king: جهاندار (‘world‑ruler/sovereign’) modifies شاه (‘king’), and بزرگ (‘great’) further qualifies شاه; the whole prepositional phrase means ‘by the fortune of the world‑ruler, the great king.’
segments.s729-g11: ازین پس کنون is a temporal phrase: ازین (از + این, ‘from this/from now’) + پس (‘after/then’) + کنون (‘now’) — an emphatic ‘from now on/at present.’ تا چه فرمان دهی is an interrogative clause: تا (‘until/now’ here functioning as a connective), چه (‘what’), فرمان (‘command/order’) and دهی is the present/subjunctive second‑person form of دادن (‘to give’), meaning ‘what orders will you give?’ (classical Persian often uses the bare stem دهی for present/subjunctive).
segments.s729-g12: This is a short noun phrase and prepositional phrase: چه آویزی (‘what pendant/hanging ornament’ — آویزی here is a noun) + از گوشوار رهی (‘from/of the traveller’s earring’). از marks source/possession ‘from/of,’ گوشوار is ‘earring,’ and رهی (‘of the wayfarer/wayfaring’) functions as a modifier, so the line asks rhetorically ‘what ornament (will come) from the wayfarer’s earring?’
segments.s729-g13: چو is the subordinating conjunction ‘when’; آن نامه is ‘that letter’; نزدیک here means ‘to/near’ and is placed before the proper name (a common classical word order), so نزدیک خسرو رسید means ‘reached near/to Khosrow’ — overall ‘when that letter reached Khosrow.’ رسید is the simple past ‘arrived/reached.’
segments.s729-g14: از آن زن ورا شادی نو رسید is a clause meaning ‘new joy arrived to him from that woman.’ از marks the source (‘from that woman’), ورا is the old pronominal/adverbial form ‘to him’ (ور + ا), شادی نو is the noun phrase ‘new joy,’ and رسید is the past verb ‘arrived/was received.’ Note the indirect object (ورا) follows the source phrase.
segments.s729-g15: فرستاده‌ای is an indefinite/periphrastic noun meaning ‘a messenger/sent one’ (the suffix -ای gives indefiniteness). خواست is the past tense of خواستن ‘he wanted/sent for’; شیرین سخن is an epithet ‘sweet‑spoken’ or ‘pleasant of speech’ placed after the verb phrase as a descriptive attribute to the messenger or the act of sending, so together: ‘he desired/sent a sweet‑spoken messenger.’
segments.s729-g16: که introduces a relative or content clause ‘who/that’; داند is the classical present third‑person singular of دانستن (‘knows’) used here where modern Persian would say می‌داند. همه داستان کهن is the object: همه (‘all’) + داستان کهن (‘the ancient tale/legend’). So the clause reads ‘who knows all the ancient tale.’
segments.s729-g33: همان is a demonstrative determiner 'that/same' modifying گنج; گنج و آن خواسته is a coordinated noun phrase 'treasure and that desired (thing)'. پیش برد here functions as a compound verbal idea 'brought forward/presented' (پیش = before/in presence of + برد = carried/took), with the object phrase placed before the verb in the poetic word order.
segments.s729-g34: یکایک is an adverb 'one by one/each and every' modifying the following clause. به + گنج‌ور marks the beneficiary/recipient 'to the treasurer'. اوبرشمرد is a fused form where او = 'him' (third‑person clitic) attaches to برشمرد (past 'counted/listed'), so literally 'he counted them for him/the treasurer.'
segments.s729-g35: This is a prepositional listing introduced by the poetic preposition ز ('of/from'): ز دینار وز گوهر شاهوار = 'in dinars and in jewels (regal)'. وز here is a contracted sequence (و + ز) functioning as 'and of' linking the two items; شاهوار is a postpositive adjective 'regal/lofty' qualifying گوهر.
segments.s729-g36: کس here functions negatively as 'no one'. آن را is the definite object ('that' + object marker). ندانست is the past negative/deficient sense 'did not know / was not able', and کردن is the light verb 'to do' used with شمار (the verbal noun 'count/tally') to mean 'to make the count' — so together کس آن را ندانست کردن شمار = 'no one knew how to perform a counting of it / no one could count it.'
segments.s729-g37: ز دیبای زر is a prepositional phrase 'from/of brocade of gold' (ز = 'from/of' in poetic register). بفت is the past 3rd‑person of بافتن 'he wove/made'; what follows (و تاج و کمر) are the objects enumerated — 'and (he wove) the crown and the girdle'. Note the object(s) and modifiers can precede or follow the verb in the elevated/poetic order.
segments.s729-g38: همان is a demonstrative referencing previously mentioned items ('that same'). تخت زرین and زرین سپر are noun + adjective phrases ('golden throne' and 'golden shield'), with زرین repeated for emphasis; the conjunction و links the two parallel phrases.
segments.s729-g39: نگه کرد is the past‑tense compound 'fixed his gaze / looked' (نگه + کرد). خسرو is the subject (proper name). بران = 'upon that' / 'on that (one)', and زاد سرو is an epithet 'cypress‑born' (زاد = 'birth/origin' used attributively with سرو 'cypress' as a metaphor for a tall, graceful youth).
segments.s729-g40: به رخ 'with regard to the face' (به used adverbially), چون بهار = 'like spring' (a simile). و links the next clause; به رفتن is a temporal/adverbial infinitive 'on/at going' and تذرو is a verb 'he scatters' — together 'and at his walking he scatters' (i.e. he scatters beauty/charm), a poetic terse clause where the object of تذرو is left implicit.
segments.s729-g41: This is a pair of prepositional similes introduced by the preposition به (be) twice: 'به رخساره روز' = 'on the face like day' and 'به گیسو چو شب' = 'on the hair like night'. چو here functions as a simile marker 'like/as'. The word order is preposition + noun (be + face/hair) followed by the simile element (روز / چو شب).
segments.s729-g42: همی before the verb marks continuous or habitual action in classical/poetic Persian. در بارد is a verbal phrase: بارد is a 3rd-person present-form of باریدن 'to fall/drip' and در here gives the sense 'onto/in'; the clause thus means 'it keeps falling onto...'. تو گویی is an idiomatic phrase meaning 'one would say / it seems as if' — گویی functions as a commentive clause; ز لب is a simple prepositional phrase 'from the lip' giving the source.
segments.s729-g43: ورا is the unstressed clitic/old-fashioned form meaning 'to her' or 'her' as the object. در شبستان is a locative phrase 'into the inner chamber/bedchamber'. فرستاد is the past tense verb 'sent' and شاه is the subject 'the king' placed after the verb (common in older Persian). The clause order here is object + locative + verb + subject.
segments.s729-g44: ز هر کس فزون شد literally 'became more/exceeded every person' — ز marks the comparison 'than', هر کس is 'everyone', فزون شد is a verbal phrase meaning 'grew/exceeded/was greater'. The second part 'و را پایگاه' is elliptical: را is the definite/object marker attached before پایگاه ('place, seat, base'), and the verb is omitted (understood from context) so the phrase implies '(and) had/was given a place'.
segments.s729-g45: فرستاد is past tense 'sent'. نزد برادرش is a prepositional phrase 'to/near his brother' (نزد means 'in the presence of' or 'to'), with the possessive suffix ش meaning 'his'. کس is the indefinite 'someone/a man' and here functions as the sent person. Word order here is verb + locative + indirect object + indefinite subject.
segments.s729-g46: همان is a demonstrative 'the same (one)'. نزد + name/title again marks direction/presence 'in the presence of / to'. دستور فریادرس is a title or name phrase (دستور here as a title/commander and فریادرس a personal name or epithet). The clause lacks an explicit verb and is elliptical: it indicates that the same person was (sent) to/appeared before Dastur Faryadres.
segments.s729-g47: بر آیین آن دین is a prepositional phrase 'according to the rites/custom of that religion' (بر + noun). رابخواست is a compound past verb 'he asked for it / he requested it' (را + خواست); او is the third-person pronoun 'him' and the small clitic مر is an archaic element present in the line — here it is part of the older pronominal/oblique sequence and does not function as modern 'to me' (its precise function is archaic/emphatic), so the phrase expresses 'he asked him for (it) according to that religion's rite.'
segments.s729-g48: بپذرفت is the past-tense verb 'he accepted'. با جان is an adverbial phrase 'with (his) life / with his soul' meaning 'wholeheartedly'. همی‌داشت (literally 'was holding/kept') describes a continuing/stateive aspect — here it conveys that he held the acceptance with his whole being; راست functions adverbially 'truly' or 'sincerely'.
segments.s729-g17: Phrase-internal order is compressed: یکی (“a certain/someone”) introduces an agent, followed by نامه (“a letter”) as the verbal object, and برسان (from رساندن) marks the sending/delivery action; ارژنگ چین is a proper name ('Aržang of Chin/China') attached at the end as the addressee. The verb that explicitly carries the past action appears in the next segment, so this segment functions as a nominative + object + infinitive/verb-stem cluster naming what was sent and to whom.
segments.s729-g18: نوشتند is simple past, third-person plural ‘they wrote’; و links clauses; کردند here means ‘they did/made’ and combines with آفرین to indicate they ‘offered/said’ praise; چند is an indefinite numeral ‘several’ modifying آفرین (an acclamation/praise). The sequence therefore reports that they wrote the letter and added/uttered several praises.
segments.s729-g19: گرانمایه is an attributive adjective ‘precious/esteemed’ modifying زن; را is the definite-object marker attaching to زن, and به درگاه خواند places the verb خواند (‘called/summoned’) at the end with the destination ‘to the court/royal presence’ (به درگاه). Word order is adjective → (object + را) → prepositional goal → verb, typical of Classical Persian.
segments.s729-g20: This clause describes a twofold action: به نامه (= the letter) is the thing acted on, ورا (= to her) is the dative; افسر ماه is a compound epithet (literally “helmet/officer of the moon” or a moon‑like title). خواند here is past tense ‘he called/named’ (i.e. ‘he called her by this epithet’). Word order places the instrument/object before the dative and the naming at the end.
segments.s729-g21: فرستاده آمد is a simple past clause ‘the messenger came’; بر زن uses the preposition بر (‘to/toward’) to indicate the recipient/place of arrival; چوگرد functions as a temporal/conjunctive particle ‘when/as’, linking this arrival to the following action. Word order: subject → verb → prepositional phrase → temporal link.
segments.s729-g22: سخنهای is the plural possessive construction ‘the words/speeches of…’; خسرو follows as the possessor (Khosrow); بدو is the dative pronoun ‘to her’; یادکرد (a fused classical form) functions as a past verbal phrase ‘recounted/related/mentioned’. The clause therefore means ‘he related Khosrow’s words to her.’
segments.s729-g23: This noun phrase stacks modifiers: زن شیر is ‘the woman (lion)’—an attributive use meaning ‘the lion‑hearted woman’ or ‘she became lion‑like’; زان (= از + آن contracted) is a prepositional compound ‘from that/because of that’; نامه شهریار is a genitive ‘the letter of the king’. Together: ‘the woman, lion‑hearted from that letter of the king…’.
segments.s729-g24: چو is the temporal/simile particle ‘when/as’; رخشنده (adjective) + گل (noun) form the simile ‘radiant as a flower’; شد is simple past ‘became’; به وقت بهار is a temporal phrase ‘at the time of spring’. The clause as a whole: ‘when she became radiant like a flower at springtime.’

sentences.s729-s13: He summoned the army to the gate and gave provisions; when the day grew bright he lifted the pack and set out.
sentences.s729-s14: When he came near the king, countless troops welcomed him.
sentences.s729-s15: When, from the road, he reached the royal court he came upon a load; the merchant's heart was filled with care.
sentences.s729-s16: After that he brought costly offerings for each one who had been with the chiefs.
sentences.s729-s25: He threw a robe upon his companions; likewise he gave dirhams, dinars, and every kind of thing.
sentences.s729-s1: The (un)chaste woman wanted an inkpot and a pen; she cast off every kind (of scruple) by the woman's counsel.
sentences.s729-s2: A certain person wrote a letter near the king, from an ill-wisher and from the well-wishing people.
sentences.s729-s3: He began the letter with praise from the very first, for the man who had washed hatred from his heart.
sentences.s729-s4: Then another matter was spoken that the king had commanded; it came to pass to the satisfaction of the well-wisher's heart.
sentences.s729-s5: The great host was scattered, by the fortune of the world-ruler, the great king.
sentences.s729-s6: From now on, then, what orders will you give; what pendant from the traveler's earring?
sentences.s729-s7: When that letter reached Khosrow, new joy came to him from that woman.
sentences.s729-s8: He sent a messenger, sweet‑spoken, who knows all the ancient tale.
sentences.s729-s17: He brought forward that same treasure and the desired things, and one by one he counted them out to the treasurer.
sentences.s729-s18: In dinars and in regal gems no one knew how to make a count of it.
sentences.s729-s19: From golden brocade he wove a crown and a girdle, and likewise the golden throne and the golden shield.
sentences.s729-s20: Khosrow fixed his gaze on that cypress‑born (youth); his face was like spring, and when he walked he scattered (beauty/splendor).
sentences.s729-s21: It falls continually on the face like day and on the hair like night; one would say it comes from your lip.
sentences.s729-s22: The king sent her into the bedchamber; she became greater than everyone and had a place/seat.
sentences.s729-s23: He sent a man to his brother; that same man (went) to the commander Faryadres.
sentences.s729-s24: According to the rites of that religion he asked him for it; he accepted and truly held it with his life.
sentences.s729-s9: They composed a letter to Aržang of Chin and wrote it, appending several praises.
sentences.s729-s10: He summoned the noble woman to the court; he read the letter to her and called her “Afser‑mah” (a moonly epithet).
sentences.s729-s11: The messenger came to the woman, and he recounted Khosrow’s words to her.
sentences.s729-s12: From that letter of the king the woman became lion‑hearted; she grew as radiant as a flower when springtime came.
